summary:
The patient exhibits a compensatory walking pattern because their weakened or paralysed quadriceps cannot properly straighten the leg during movement; thus, they use other nearby joints instead. This aligns with option \textbf{(C)} being accurate regarding the affected muscle group involved in such gait abnormalities seen post-polio infection. Agent1 summarized that:

The primary cause of hand-to-knee gait in individuals recovering from poliomyelitis involves weakness or paralysis affecting the **quadriceps**—the main extensor of the thigh at the knee joint—which forces alternative movements involving more proximal musculature like those used when moving towards knees while using hands as support. Thus, focusing directly on identifying compromised muscular function centralizes around understanding how specific neuromuscular deficits manifest clinically through altered gaits indicative of functional limitations imposed by neurological damage sustained earlier stages of disease progression characterized here specifically within context provided about options available among choices given initially posed query concerning etiology behind particular type of ambulatory behavior noted amongst afflicted subjects suffering late effects associated closely linked historically speaking broadly across populations exposed previously either acutely or chronically over time relative incidence rates reported globally varying widely depending upon geographical location socioeconomic status public health infrastructure etc... 

In essence,
- Weakness/paralysis impacting *Quadriceps* leads
to characteristic 'hand-to-knee'
gait adaptation;
